passages.explanation: Some men come from Judea to Antioch teaching that unless Gentile believers are circumcised according to Mosaic law, they can't be saved. Paul and Barnabas have sharp dispute and debate with them. The church sends Paul, Barnabas, and others to Jerusalem to settle this with the apostles and elders. On the way they pass through Phoenicia and Samaria, reporting the conversion of the Gentiles, which brings great joy. In Jerusalem, some believers from the Pharisee party stand up and insist: the Gentiles must be circumcised and required to keep the law of Moses. This is the defining theological crisis of the early church — is faith in Jesus enough, or must Gentiles become Jews first?

The apostles and elders meet to consider this question. After much discussion, Peter stands up and reminds them: God chose him to be the one through whom the Gentiles heard the gospel and believed. God, who knows the heart, showed he accepted them by giving them the Holy Spirit, just as he did to us — making no distinction. Why are you testing God by putting a yoke on the Gentiles' necks that neither we nor our ancestors could bear? We believe it's through the grace of the Lord Jesus that we are saved, just as they are. Peter's argument is experiential and theological: God already decided this at Cornelius' house, and the law was a burden even for Jews.

The whole assembly falls silent and listens to Barnabas and Paul describe the signs and wonders God did among the Gentiles through them. Then James — the Lord's brother and leader of the Jerusalem church — speaks. He cites Amos 9:11-12: the rebuilt tent of David will cause the rest of humanity to seek the Lord, including all the Gentiles who bear God's name. His judgment: don't make it difficult for the Gentiles turning to God. Instead, write telling them to abstain from food polluted by idols, from sexual immorality, from the meat of strangled animals, and from blood. These four restrictions aren't the full Mosaic law — they're practical boundaries that allow Jewish and Gentile believers to share table fellowship without violating the deepest Jewish sensibilities.

The apostles, elders, and whole church decide to send chosen men to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas, carrying a letter. They choose Judas called Barsabbas and Silas, leading men among the believers. The letter is diplomatic and clear: we've heard that some went out from us without our authorisation and disturbed you. We've unanimously decided to send representatives along with our dear friends Barnabas and Paul, who have risked their lives for the name of the Lord Jesus Christ. It seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us not to burden you beyond these essentials: abstain from food sacrificed to idols, from blood, from strangled animals, and from sexual immorality. The phrase 'it seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us' is remarkable — divine guidance working through communal deliberation.

The delegation goes to Antioch, gathers the church, and delivers the letter. The people read it and are glad for its encouraging message. Judas and Silas, who are prophets, encourage and strengthen the believers with many words. After spending some time there, they're sent off in peace. Paul and Barnabas remain in Antioch, teaching and preaching the word of the Lord with many others. The crisis is resolved. Gentile freedom is affirmed. The church holds together.

After some time, Paul suggests to Barnabas that they revisit every city where they preached to see how the believers are doing. Barnabas wants to take John Mark. Paul disagrees sharply — Mark deserted them in Pamphylia and shouldn't come. The disagreement is so severe that they part company. Barnabas takes Mark and sails for Cyprus. Paul chooses Silas and heads through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the churches. The split is painful but providential: two missionary teams instead of one. And Mark isn't finished — Paul will later call him useful (2 Timothy 4:11). Restoration takes time, but it happens.
